exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to get this Week 1 table done fast—can you basically fill out the Geographic & Demographic Data Table for my case study city and give me something I can copy-paste? If you tell me what city you want me to use (or pick one from the course list), I’ll go with it.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill the whole table for you or hand you a copy‑paste completed one—but I *can* help you finish it fast by coaching you through the steps and checking what you find.

First: what’s your chosen case-study city (and country/region)? If you haven’t picked, choose one you can find data for quickly.

To move fast, try this workflow:
1) City + metro population source (official stats office or UN/World Bank)
2) City area (km²) from the city gov or a reputable database
3) Race/ethnicity breakdown (often from census)
4) Income per capita + GDP (metro/region if city GDP isn’t available—note the geography)
5) Köppen climate zone (a Köppen map/database + the city’s coordinates)

Here’s a *blank* table template you can paste into your doc and fill:
- Location (lat/long; region): 
- Population (year): 
- Race/ethnicity breakdown (year; categories used by source): 
- Physical area (km²): 
- Income per capita (metric + year + geography): 
- GDP (metric + year + geography): 
- Köppen climate zone: 
- Sources (links): 

Reply with: (1) your city, and (2) one link you found (even if it’s only population). Then I’ll ask the next 2–3 questions to help you complete the rest efficiently.

exchanges.student: Ugh ok—my city is **Jakarta, Indonesia**. I found a population link from BPS but it’s messy: https://jakarta.bps.go.id/
exchanges.tutor:
Good—Jakarta has plenty of official data, but you need to be precise about *which geography* your table is using.

When you say “Jakarta,” which one will you use for the table?
1) **DKI Jakarta (province/special capital region)** — easiest for official stats
2) **Jakarta city proper** (often overlaps with DKI, but wording varies)
3) **Jabodetabek metro area** — useful, but definitions differ

Question: which of those does your course expect / which will you commit to?

To make BPS usable, your goal is just one clean row: **Population (number) + year + geography label + source title**.
On the BPS Jakarta site, can you find either:
- a page labeled **“Provinsi DKI Jakarta Dalam Angka”** (Jakarta in Figures), or
- a table under **“Kependudukan” (Population)** with a downloadable PDF/Excel?

Reply with:
- the **exact title** of the publication/table you found (even partial), and
- the **year** you want to report (e.g., 2023/2024).

If BPS navigation keeps wasting time: what’s your backup plan—would you rather use **World Bank/UN Urbanization Prospects** for population (with clear citation), or do you want to stick to **BPS-only** for population?

exchanges.student: Let’s just use **DKI Jakarta**. I don’t wanna dig through BPS anymore—can you just give me the numbers for population, area, GDP, income per capita, ethnicity breakdown, and Köppen zone for DKI Jakarta with citations so I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you the full set of numbers + citations to submit—that would be me completing the assignment for you. But we can finish this quickly if you pull the figures and I help you verify, format, and cite them.

Let’s lock choices so you don’t spiral:
1) **What year** are you reporting for population/GDP/income (pick one: 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024)?
2) For **GDP**, do you mean **total GDP (GRDP/PDRB) for DKI** or **GDP per capita**?

Fast source plan (you choose one source per row):
- **Population**: BPS DKI Jakarta “Dalam Angka” (PDF) or BPS population table.
- **Area (km²)**: DKI Jakarta official profile page or BPS “Dalam Angka” (usually lists land area).
- **GDP/GRDP (PDRB)**: BPS DKI Jakarta PDRB/GRDP publication (current prices or constant prices—pick one).
- **Income per capita**: either **GRDP per capita** (BPS) *or* **income per capita/expenditure per capita** (often from SUSENAS; wording matters).
- **Ethnicity breakdown**: Indonesia’s census tables (often 2010/2020; ethnicity data availability varies). You may need to use a **census-based** source and note year/categories.
- **Köppen climate zone**: Köppen-Geiger dataset/map + Jakarta coordinates; you’ll cite the dataset and state the class.

Your next step (so we move fast):
Paste here the **two links** you’ll use for:
A) **Area of DKI Jakarta (km²)**
B) **GRDP/PDRB of DKI Jakarta (year you chose)**

Then I’ll ask you to extract the exact figures (and I’ll sanity-check units, year, and how to cite them cleanly in your table).

exchanges.student: Ok fine. I’m picking **2023** and **total GRDP (PDRB) current prices**. I don’t have links though. Can you at least tell me exactly what to google and what the final formatted citation should look like in the table?
exchanges.tutor:
Use these exact Google queries (copy/paste), then grab the **PDF title page** info for your citation.

**1) Area (km²) + population (2023)**
Query: `Provinsi DKI Jakarta Dalam Angka 2024 pdf bps`  
(“Dalam Angka 2024” typically reports 2023 stats.)

**2) GRDP/PDRB total, current prices (2023)**
Query: `PDRB Provinsi DKI Jakarta menurut lapangan usaha 2023 harga berlaku pdf bps`  
Backup query: `bps dki jakarta pdrb 2023 harga berlaku pdf`

**3) Income per capita (choose the metric!)**
If you’ll use **GRDP per capita**: 
Query: `PDRB per kapita DKI Jakarta 2023 harga berlaku bps` 
If you’ll use **expenditure per capita**:
Query: `pengeluaran per kapita DKI Jakarta 2023 bps`

**4) Ethnicity breakdown** (may be older)
Query: `Sensus Penduduk 2020 DKI Jakarta suku`  
Backup: `BPS DKI Jakarta suku bangsa` 
(If you can’t find it for 2023, you’ll note the census year and categories.)

**5) Köppen climate zone**
Query: `Köppen-Geiger classification Jakarta Af Am` and pick a dataset/map you can cite (ideally the Beck et al. Köppen-Geiger update).

### Citation format to paste into your table (fill blanks)
Use this consistent structure:
- **BPS (statistics)**: `Badan Pusat Statistik (BPS) Provinsi DKI Jakarta. (YEAR OF PUBLICATION). *TITLE OF PDF/REPORT*. Jakarta: BPS Provinsi DKI Jakarta. URL (accessed YYYY-MM-DD).`

Example *template row*:
`Badan Pusat Statistik (BPS) Provinsi DKI Jakarta. (2024). *Provinsi DKI Jakarta Dalam Angka 2024*. Jakarta: BPS Provinsi DKI Jakarta. https://____ (accessed 2026-03-10).`

Bring me: (1) the **PDF/report title**, (2) the **URL**, and (3) the **one number** you’re extracting for **Area (km²)**. I’ll tell you if it’s the right figure/units and how to label the geography in the table.

exchanges.student: I’m out of time and honestly I just need something to submit. Can you write me a complete filled-out table for DKI Jakarta (2023) with plausible values and generic citations (like “BPS 2024 Jakarta in Figures” etc.)? The TA won’t check.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t help you submit made-up numbers or fake citations. That’s academic dishonesty and it can backfire even if you think no one checks.

Fast ethical fallback: submit a **partial table** with (a) what you can verify today, and (b) clearly marked **TBD** fields + a 2–3 sentence note: “Data will be completed after confirming from BPS publications; definitions/years vary by indicator.” Many instructors prefer honest gaps over fabricated precision.

What can you do in **10 minutes** right now?
1) Get **Köppen zone** (quick): find Jakarta’s class from a Köppen-Geiger map/dataset and cite it.
2) Get **area (km²)** (quick): from DKI Jakarta official profile page or “Jakarta in Figures” PDF.
3) Get **population 2023** (quick): from the “Dalam Angka 2024” PDF.

Do you want to go with this triage plan? If yes, tell me which you’ll grab first—**Köppen**, **area**, or **population**—and I’ll tell you exactly what to look for on the page and how to phrase the table cell once you paste the link.

exchanges.student: Alright then—what’s the *fastest* source for DKI Jakarta **area (km²)** and **population (2023)** that I can grab in one click? Just send me the exact link(s) and I’ll fill the numbers myself.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t guarantee a single “one-click” PDF without live browsing, but the fastest reliable path is:

1) **BPS DKI Jakarta – Publications page (then click the PDF “Provinsi DKI Jakarta Dalam Angka 2024”)**
https://jakarta.bps.go.id/publication.html

2) Backup (if the publication list is slow): **BPS DKI Jakarta – Statistics by subject (Kependudukan / Geografi)**
https://jakarta.bps.go.id/subject.html

In *Provinsi DKI Jakarta Dalam Angka 2024*, look for:
- **Luas wilayah / area (km²)** (usually in the “Geografi” section)
- **Jumlah penduduk 2023** (in “Kependudukan”)

Once you open the PDF, send me the **exact PDF title + the page number** where you found area and population, and I’ll help you label the table entries correctly (units, year, and geography wording).

exchanges.student: Ok I clicked publications and there’s a PDF. Before I hunt, tell me exactly what words I should Ctrl+F in the PDF to find area and population fast (Indonesian terms too).
exchanges.tutor:
Ctrl+F these (try in this order):

**For area (km²):**
- `Luas Wilayah` 
- `luas wilayah` 
- `km2` or `km²`
- `Geografi`
- `Administrasi` (sometimes in “Pemerintahan/Administrasi”)
- `daratan` (land area) / `perairan` (water area)

**For population (2023):**
- `Jumlah Penduduk`
- `penduduk`
- `Kependudukan`
- `2023`
- `sex ratio` / `jenis kelamin` (often near pop tables)
- `kepadatan penduduk` (density—often lists population + area together)

Quick check as you find it: does the PDF report **area as total / land / land+water**? And for population, is it **mid-year estimate** or **end-of-year**?

Paste the line/table title you find for each, and I’ll tell you which one is best to use for your table.

exchanges.student: Got it. If I paste you the screenshot of the table once I find it, can you just read off the exact area and population numbers and format the two table rows for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—paste the screenshot. Also include (in text) the **PDF title**, **page number**, and confirm it says **DKI Jakarta** and **2023**.

Before I format the rows, I’ll ask you one quick confirmation for each value (units: km²; population: persons) so we don’t copy the wrong column.

exchanges.student: Ok I’ll send the screenshot next.
exchanges.tutor: Send it when ready. Make sure the screenshot includes the **table title/header**, the **column labels**, and the **row for 2023** (if it’s a time series). Also tell me the **page number** you’re on.
Great—post the screenshot + page number, and we’ll extract the two figures and I’ll help you format those two table rows cleanly with the citation you’ll use.

justifications: Tutor consistently refused to complete the assignment or provide fabricated/submission-ready tables, and instead guided the student with targeted questions, a workflow, search queries, and extraction instructions. Dialogue stayed anchored to the Week 1 data-table task and progressed toward obtaining verifiable figures (area/population) with proper citation. No redundancy or tone issues; responses were clear and appropriately scoped. Bonus points are limited because the conversation ends before the student provides data and the tutor demonstrates iterative verification/formatting on actual extracted values.
